entry.title: Condition Contract - Fixed Amount Distribution in Settlement Document
entry.content:
Dear Expert,

In condition contract, percentage base rebate calculation work as per expectation. It is considering negative value such as credit note or cancel invoice value for rebate base value and accrual also updated in negative amount.

But in case of fixed about based rebate calculation, it's considering all value for rebate base value but accruals is always positive. So, I want to know if this is a standard behavior of fixed amount-based rebate calculation or through configuration/customization we can avoid considering negative value.

Thanks,

Arindam

entry.title: Contingent Worker ID Generation does not work as before
entry.content:
Dear Community,

Contingent Worker ID Generation does not work as before, Emp ID with CW prefix was generated. Now only a number is generated.

After 1H2024 Release, it was possible to opting out of the feature "Enable Centralized Services to Add Contingent Worker and Add Assignment" in Company System and Logo Settings, the Employee ID of Contingent Workers was generated as per the business rule again.

Now it is not possible to opt out "Enable Centralized Services to Add Contingent Worker and Add Assignment" feature. Currently User ID Generation for Contingent Worker follows the "Next Employee ID Assigned" in Company System and Logo Settings. However it does not support alphanumeric values having the CW prefix. 

Do you have the same issue? Have you found any solution to fix this problem? I opened a SAP support ticket and the agent said that there is no solution for this issue. But maybe there is a special workaround what can help to keep the original settings and continue using CW prefix in the Employee ID of the CWs.

Thanks.
Regards,
Szabi

entry.title: Re: How to convert binary base64 string to BMP image and upload to SE78 Tx. through program
entry.content:
Just to be clear, your string of characters in base64 was used to encode a PNG image (based on what you have said). You may encode any sequence of bytes into Base64, it's not related to any format, see here: Base64 - Wikipedia.

So, your question is how to convert PNG to BMP. Try IGS -> https://www.google.com/search?q=abap+convert+png+to+bmp+-jpeg+-jpg+site%3Asap.com.

entry.title: Re: How To Install an SAP HANA Express 2.0 SPS04 VM server for development (on the budget)
entry.content:
write a comment for this "Introduction I'm a software developer so from time to time I have some weird & geek activities at home. Last one was to install my own SAP HANA Express virtual machine at home and I want to share the experience because I'm sure there is some interest about this. Why SAP HANA 2.0 for development ? Because it's the most easy way to have a running platform all-in-one for development with In-memory database with persistency Cloud Foundry landscape Integrated Web-IDE as a running appliance SAML2 identity provider for security You can develop in any programming language supported by Cloud Foundry & CF community You have a running Linux machine with bash shell I downloaded the SAP HANA Express Virtual Appliance (hxexsa.ova file & gettting started pdf) using SAP download java program after registering minimal informations about myself (here) and I imported the appliance to my existing HP DL380 G5 server that was running VMWare ESXi 5.5 (free licence) Following the provided "Getting Started" document I had errors on the first boot regarding some : missing instruction in the CPU. Of course, I searched for help about this and I found some information about it but none of them solved my problem. SAP HANA Express supported hardware Searching the internet I found the official website for hardware compatibility here Unfortunately the list does not provide information about CPU features used by SAP HANA and what is the minimum requirement for version 2.0 SPS04 to run. Using CPU World database compare, I found out the differences between my CPU and the minimum Intel supported platform : SSE4.2 instruction set Another important info that I learned is : SAP HANA is supported on minimum Intel E5-2600 v3 and Power 8 platforms only ! (no AMD processor is supported) So I needed an complete new server with a supported CPU and as you may know servers are expensive and not really for home use as I needed for my development mahine. I searched a lot of time until I found the most cost effective server that is able to run SAP HANA Express virtual appliance : Dell R630 with Intel E5-2630L v3 processors found on Ebay For 590 GBP I got : Dell R630 with 2 x Intel E5-2630L-v3 (8 core) processors and 64 GB of RAM. For HDDs I installed an NVMe SSD drive (HP EX920 512GB) using M.2 PCIe x4 adapter (from Delock) mounted in PCIe Riser 3 card and used for fast storage (HANA VM imported here) and fast datastore for OS made of 2 x Samsung Evo860 500GB SSD configured in RAID1, and really cheap refurbished 2 x Samsung 1TB 2.5" 5400rpm drives configured in one RAID1 volume just for backup. Host OS (VM Ware ESXi 6.7u3) I choose the ESXi 6.7u3 because it's free and it has no RAM limit in the latest version for 2 processor servers. It has a very nice web interface, and most of all it has ready to use images with drivers by the hardware vendors (both Dell and HPE have for download pre-configured versions for their servers) which is great because they are easy to download and install. It can be downloaded from the hardware support portal of your server supplier or directly from VMWare Tips for new NVMe PCIe drives & ESXi 6.7u3 be sure that your PCIe lanes are configured properly (for Dell R630 the BIOS has PCIe bifurcation which needs to be set to "x4 x4 x4 x4" and the drive has to be in Riser 3 or 2) not all NVMe drives are visible and accesible from boot, some of them work with OS level drivers (HP EX920 included) If NVMe drive controller is not visible you might need to copy the nvme.v00 file from v6.5u2 iso to v6.7u3 iso after burning or flashing the OS install drive (more info here) Importing VM with OVF Tool Because the hxexsa.ova image file it has over 15GB, the best way to import the virtual machine is to use the command line OVF Tool ovftool --disableVerification --noSSLVerify --datastore=datasatoreName --network="VM Network" hxexsa.ova vi://root@esxi.yourdomain.com Official OVF Tool help may be found here Using HXE VM is easy There is a pretty good start-up guide of almost 20 pages of how to start your HANA experience when you download the image, but there are a lot of example apps and tutorials published by SAP : https://developers.sap.com/topics/sap-hana.html https://github.com/SAP/hana-shine-xsa/tree/HANA2.0-SPS03 Conclusions As a developer or an small ISV company you can have your own SAP HANA 2.0 SPS04 platform running on cheap hardware for play. I managed to get up and running for development an SAP HANA Express Virtual Machine with less than 1,000 EUR budget for hardware & software. Use Intel CPUs supported, minimum E5-2600 v3 family for server and at least 16GB or RAM (32GB will run better) even if the VM works on new i7 & i9 desktop & laptop machines. Did I mentioned that SAP HANA Express Edition is available for free, for development and productive use, up to 32GB of RAM?"

Thanks for sharing your experience with setting up SAP HANA Express on your home server! It's always inspiring to see how fellow developers tackle such geeky projects. Your detailed guide on hardware compatibility and your personal journey from initial setup challenges to finding a cost-effective solution is incredibly helpful.

It's great to know that you've managed to get SAP HANA Express up and running with a budget under 1,000 EUR. Your insights on choosing the Dell R630 with Intel E5-2630L v3 processors and the use of NVMe SSD drives are particularly useful. I also appreciate the tips on configuring PCIe lanes and dealing with NVMe drives visibility issues in ESXi 6.7u3.

For anyone looking to set up their own SAP HANA development environment, your experience and advice will definitely be invaluable. Kudos on a job well done, and thanks again for sharing all these resources and tips!

entry.title: Re: Does SAP SLT support Azure SQL Database as a source
entry.content:
Microsoft Azure is explicitly stated as not supported with SLT in 1768805 - SAP Landscape Transformation Replication Server (SLT): Non ABAP-based Sources: "Especially Microsoft Azure is NOT supported (see SAP Note 2497147)."

There is no workaround for this.

entry.title: High level decription of Brazilian eSocial processing
entry.content:
Processing steps
 
1. Data gathering
Every eSocial event has its own Data Processing/Generation class (SE24). They can be identified in view V_T7BREFD_EVTYPE (standard) or V_T7BREFD_EVTY_C (customer) for each event. The customer table overwrites the standard table entries, if the same key exists.
 
Example entry of event S-1010 with processing class
 
The purpose of this class is to read data from the system and progressively 'assemble' the event data in the given layout. The layout is provided by the standard system for all events, including 'out of HCM scope' events that the customer must implement in the Framework, for example SST or Worker Process events. More info on how to create custom events in the attachments of note 1920686 - eSocial: FAQ.
So, in order to identify data or conditions problems, the best place to look is the processing class. All events are generated by calling the common method 'Process', when this step is finished all the data is filled in the event record, one record per event.
Inside the Processing class all the event customer BAdIs are called in order to compose parts of the event where the standard normally doesn't have the data within HCM.
For all 3 types of eSocial events (table, periodic, non periodic) this is the same. There are different generation programs for the types, but every event has its own Processing Class with roughly the same structure.
 
2. Change Manager
When all events are generated by the Processing class above, the records are passed to the 'Change Manager'. The event key is compared to existing events already in the database, and depending on the status, the system takes an approach:
a. The event has no correspondent key. A new event is generated in order to be sent.
b. The event has an existing key. If the existing event:
Is not sent or has an error, the new one takes its place to be sent to the government.
Is sent and has receipt, the new one is created as a rectification and it will be sent again.
c. If the generator has NOT created an event for the period, and the event already exists and was sent for that period:
The system creates a new deletion event to be sent.
 
The Change Manager also utilizes the Conversion Class, also listed in view V_T7BREFD_EVTYPE (standard) or V_T7BREFD_EVTY_C (customer), in order to avoid generating rectifications in special cases, for example in eSocial layout changes. There are standard conversion classes but the customer can copy/customize them if needed.
So, if you are wondering why some event is being rectified, deleted or updated, this step can be investigated in detail. Most of the logic is standard, with the exception of the customer created Conversion Class for this processing.
 
3. XML Generation, Signature and Sending
The standard solution provides transformations, XML generation, signature and sending for all events including the 'out of HCM scope'. The customer events, if properly integrated in the framework, will be handled the same as the standard events.
Certificates must be imported in the system for event signature, more details in the blog post HCM Brazil eSocial: Connecting and testing your digital certificates for eSocial SOA Manager 
The eSocial communication package is implemented in SOAManager, more info:
  • SAP Note 2514483 - eSocial - SOA Manager - configuration of web service for communication to eSocial restricted production environment.
  • SAP Note 2585709 - eSocial - SOA Manager - configuration of web service for communication to eSocial production environment.
 
I hope this helps for troubleshooting some technical problems that might arise in the eSocial day to day use.

entry.title: Re: HANA - /hana/shared//xs/app_working//executionroot fo...
entry.content:
I try using a HANA cockpit server, in my case i use this note 2775467 - XSA - Huge log files are being generated in "/hana/<...>/executionroot" folder - Best Practices for analysis - SAP for Me help me to fix it.

Add the following property to the xscontroller.ini file, under the [general] section:

Controller.Balancer.MaxStoredDroplets=1
  
Note: Reducing MaxStoredDroplets will reduce the possibility of further log analysis as no old logs (e.g. from previous crashes) will be available.

Working better, remove or move files was not an option for my case.

When the payment proposal is created, this payment data is lost and payment needs to be reallocated again.

The error shown in exceptions is No Valid Payment Method Found

In app Manage Automatic Payments in View Log the following is displayed 


goto View log herein you will see the following stated 
> House bank is selected ...
> No suitable bank can be selected for comp.code & pmnt meth. & curr. &  (& in place of actual values shown)

entry.title: Re: Automatic payments – payment data is not transferred...
entry.content:
See referenced in the help.sap.com documentation 

Manage Automatic Payments: FAQ
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/031c345485b84c8c94265be9ef61d3a8/0bf4918dd83a4e92bc87fc575166e93f.html?locale=en-US

No Valid Payment Method Found or other errors related to payment method.
The reason for this can be wrong combination of payment method and house bank accounts, incomplete configuration settings, missing of certain parameters, etc. For more information about payment method related errors and trouble shooting, see SAP Knowledge Base Article 2671294 Information published on SAP site.

In this note you will see reference to Configuration activity  Set Up Bank Determination for Payment Transactions id 101045

..

Configuration of the Payment Method with Bank:

Configuration activity: Set Up Bank Determination for Payment Transactions (ID 101045) Ranking Order and Bank Accounts.
Ranking Order: You specify which house banks are permitted and rank them in a list. The smaller the number, the higher the rank.
This process includes the following steps:
a) Rank the house banks based on the payment method and the payment currency.
b) Specify the accounts and the subaccounts for the house banks

Bank Accounts: For each house bank and payment method (and currency, if required), you specify which bank account is to be used for payments.
..

Review note in full.

entry.title: Re: Process order settlement with actual quantity
entry.content:
Hi @SMuthukumar ,

Normally, during the Preliminary Settlement for Co-Products (CO8A), no costs should be posted into the coproducts (order items) without output. This ensures you don't get any variances and price differences (PRD) for these coproducts.

Please check the default rule you are using for your order type/plant (OPL8). Please check the help guidance for this. Eg. PP6 default rule will probably be the best option for you.

I hope this helps.

BR,

Filipe

entry.title: Re: Variable Pay Bonus Payout - standard fields
entry.content:
Hi @S0026446342 

From your screenshot you are not getting the Individual Section Amount to calculate which is a prerequisite for Goals payouts to calculate.

 

Path in VP is : Basis field in Employee History gets prorated to make the Target Bonus amount of each assignment, that amount then gets split based on Bonus Plan Section Weights to make up the Section Amounts, then the Section Amount gets split into Goal Basis based on goal weight (business or individual). If any of the steps below is missing, there is no payout calculated for a goal.

entry.title: Re: Condition Contract - Fixed Amount Distribution in Set...
entry.content:
Hi @arindamsur, 

The behavior you’re observing is standard in SAP CCM for calculating a fixed amount (calculation type B) in the settlement document. This is driven by the standard pricing routine 214 assigned to the rebate condition in the pricing procedure, specifically the logic within the following method:

If you’re considering modifying this logic, I strongly recommend conducting thorough regression testing. Adjusting this standard calculation may lead to unintended issues, such as complications when the settlement includes only negative line item business volumes (BVs).

That said, from a broader perspective, SAP ensures that the fixed amount is calculated and posted correctly at the document level. This behavior aligns with the standard design of SAP CCM.


Regards, 
Gauthier Victor

entry.title: SAP PAPM Cloud Universal Model: Importance of Function Sequence
entry.content:
Hi Everyone

I am here again to share with you this time a very short blogpost of an important functionality with respect to SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud, Universal Model (SAP PAPM Cloud UM).

If you are a modeler, most likely you already have encountered and have seen the Function Sequence buttons such as Up, Down, First, Last.

 

At first glance you might assume that these buttons are purely for visual and organizational point of view. But did you know that these are powerful buttons that can stir your model's logical calculation result and function trigger priorities?

 

For example, let me ask you a simple question. 

In Collection, if you add several functions as inputs, which one will be triggered or called first upon run action?
Some of you will answer "the first collection input", maybe some would say "alphabetical". 
but of course some of you answered it right --- "It depends on function sequence". 😊

Yes you got it right! Function sequence sets the priority of the functions and provides more clarity and guidance for the system to identify which must be triggered first specially in a complex model that could have a "chicken or egg" or complex predecessors situation. 

 

Let me give you one of the very simple scenarios where we can already encounter the right use of Function sequence. 

ℹ️ Additional Information
The screen you are seeing below is from Universal Model called Model Screen which provides visual modeling experience to the modelers just like in Standard Model. But that is for another blogpost, so please stay tuned! 😉

As you can see, the simple environment above shows a conflicting sitution.
How did I say so? If you look at the predecessor functions of Join UnionAll - 03, it says here that Join Unionall - 01 and View 01 are of the same distance (Distance 02) but at the same time View 01 is tagged as Distance 03 and is actually an input of Join UnionAll - 01.

With such situation, how would Join UnionAll - 01 behave? As they are also of the same distance, Join UnionAll - 01 will expect an already triggered result of View 01 and will use the buffered data. This is nothing unusual and for seasoned modelers, you have already experienced this. Remember something like...

"I triggered the function and the result was ok. After changing a formula of such function, reactivate, and run the full chain, the result being produced still comes from the old run" 

or something like

"I executed a run of the final function in the chain, and it did not produce a result. But if i run the predecessor functions one by one, then trigger the final function - the result was correct" 

 

These are not system nor run inconsistencies, but something that we as usual modelers should always put in mind ---- for such situations, please do not forget to recheck your function sequence. 

 

Before I explain further let us simplify the terms, since it might get confusing to discuss the topics by mentioning all functions I have, let us  call it green stream

and red stream

Since we already know that the green stream will just "read" the buffered data of View 01, we need to ensure that the red stream is called in first when executing. Let me show you what will happen if green stream is called in first in the Join UnionAll - 03 function.

 

As you can see in Manage Functions , View 02 has an earlier sequence number, with such upon run action of Join UnionAll - 03, it will consider green stream first prior the red

Due to this, without knowing the logic behind my modeling, you would notice that Model Entity 02 has been called in both Red and Green stream. And since I am just using UnionAll, we should expect two set of entries from Model Entity 02 (Those with Customer and Product starting with ME2). But you can see that this is missing in Green stream.

This just proves, that this chain reads buffered data from View 01. And since it was never executed yet and had no buffered data, it returned an empty result from Model Entity 02 > View 01 stream.

What if we make sure that red stream has earlier functions sequence number than of green?

⚠️ Reminder
Make sure to reactivate your environment after changing function sequence

 

Here is the result of the run -- one additional line!! Just a simple visual representation that the green stream produced the right result (now with proper buffered View 01 produced data) 

 

 

Alright, as I promised this will be a very simple and fast blogpost! I hope you have better appreciation now with the Up, Down, First, Last buttons. 😊 Bye for now and happy modeling in the cloud!

.

.

.

. 

 

ℹ️Additional Information

There are functions that are automatically considered to be triggered and will always produce new set of data regardless of the sequence and complexity of the environment to ensure proper results will be provided.  

These functions are automatically executed with Application Server's attention:
-- Writers (with or without connection)
-- Reader (to replicate data for Odata and SAC)
-- Iterative View

entry.title: PR/PO Creation with Mix of Service and Material Items
entry.content:
We have a requirement of creating PR/PO with the mixing of Service and Material items. 

Currently, we mapped the CIG of PurchaseOrderExport with the validity start date & validity end date of the first item to the header. 
Therefore, we have the limitation that we need always to put the service item as the first item, otherwise it will fail. 
However, we need to find a way to successfully create PR/PO with this combination with any order. We have tried mapping the validity start date & validity end date with for-each where the validity start date & validity end date are not empty. This works when we have only one service item but fails when we have multiple service items as it sends (StartDateOfServiceItem1,StartDateOfServiceItem2,...) to the header level (which can accept only one value).

entry.title: Re: Compensation Performance Monitoring Tools
entry.content:
The customer should discuss this with the Customer success manager from the cloud success services. MCC can conduct some investigations to understand/replicate the behavior. 

Continuous Quality Check Interface Management (CQC IFM) could help understand better the sitaution.

The CQC Interface Management service is available for solutions that are either in production or close to
production start up. It focuses on hybrid scenarios connecting cloud applications with on-premise systems. Worth to mention is that the CQC Interface Management service focuses on the analysis of SAP components.
Processing steps that may be conducted on third-party sender/receiver systems or third-party middleware hubs cannot be analyzed.

But as Xavier mentions most of the time it's caused by configuration and there are some improvements they can make.

entry.title: SAP Business AI: Crossing the Chasm
entry.content:
It was a little more than a year ago I penned the blog "Why SAP Joule is a different attempt this time?". Fast forward to today, it's evident that Joule is the most significant AI offering from SAP in recent years.

On one hand, it was all good and great with waves of SAP Business AI capabilities showcased in SAP SAPPHIRE and TechEd. On the other hand, there is a growing murmur of "AI Fatigue" among customers. Amidst the "AI Hype", where do we stand with the SAP Business AI adoption now?

If we use Gartner's "Hype Cycle", through this year, the "AI Hype" has likely passed the "Peak of Inflated Expectation" and is edging towards "Disillusionment". What does it mean for the Business AI adoption as a new technology? When the Hype Cycle maps to the "Diffusion of Innovations" (from Everett Rogers): instead of a smooth transition, there is a "Chasm" (by Geoffrey A. Moore) emerges between the "Early Adopters" to the "Early Majority". What is a Chasm? It is a critical gap or barrier that many high-tech products face when transitioning from the early adopters (enthusiastic, risk-tolerant consumers) to the early majority (more pragmatic, risk-averse consumers) in the Technology Adoption Life Cycle.

Diagram 1: Hype Cycle and Technology Adoption Lifecycle Plotted Together

SAP Joule and the Business AI are gaining traction among "Early Adopters", but how can they bridge the chasm to reach the "Early Majority"? In the book "Crossing the Chasm", Moore offers several recommendations, to put them in the context of SAP Business AI:

  • Comprehensive Coverage of Capabilities: This may be the natural one and SAP is diligently expanding the AI offerings with more out-of-box AI capabilities and also the extensions available. This mirrors the evolution of Fiori (firstly introduced about a decade ago!), customers had challenges selecting from the limited Fiori app library to nowadays "Fiori as default".

  • Building Credibility Through Success Stories: This is the more challenging one, why? Because there is a long existing stereotype that by the "Pace Layering" framework, SAP is often viewed as the "System of Records" which is with infrequent changes and need stability. That is correct in the context of traditional Business Suite era: 5+ years between a major upgrade was normal practice. The On-Premise solution release cycle was counted by years, with the custom codes as technical debt, it became very difficult to make a business case to justify a million-dollar upgrade even yearly, not to mention in months.

Diagram 2: Pace Layering Framework

 

Things started to change with the Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) competitors occurring in the “Differentiation” layer with Line of Business (LoB) specific solutions in the Sales, then Finance and Human Resources eroding the Business Suite market. Additionally, the large Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) providers disrupted the software development, deployment and operations. In response, SAP acquired LoB SaaS solution providers and then SAP introduced HANA Cloud Platform which evolved into todays “Business Technology Platform” (BTP) to tackle the “innovation” tier.

However, introducing new technologies is easier than changing perceptions. For SAP Business AI adoption, raising awareness is crucial. As Enterprise Architects, we’ve experimented with various approaches, and I found the following one worked relatively well:

Diagram 3: AI Adoption Approach with SAP Premium Engagement

 

  • "Myth Buster" Session (1 hour): Supported by the SAP AI Centre of Expertise, this session will provide the basic knowledge of the SAP AI capabilities and a high-level overview the potentially relevant use case.

  • Architect Point of View (aPoV) Workshops (2 days): For customers seeking deeper understanding and live demos, these workshops offer a holistic view - from embedded AI (e.g., Machine Learning) to the latest Generative AI capabilities. They include live demos and preliminary use case discussions. Feedback indicates that customers are often surprised by the available capabilities and their ease of application.

  • Identifying AI Use Cases: This can be challenging due to mismatched expectations and technological realities. The nuances sometimes can be road blockers afterwards. The tools like SAP Signavio can provide scenario recommendations based on the systems statistics. In this part we advise customers not only thinking of a high-level story but also break down the use cases into Business View, Technical View and Ethical View, utilising templates from SAP AppHaus like below.

Table 1: AI Scenario Formulation Sentence by SAP AppHaus

 

  • Applying Out-of-box AI capabilities: There are Machine Learning or GenAI capabilities ready to use from either S/4HANA or other SAP solutions. The reality is often less straightforward. Customers frequently face blockers because of the lower S/4HANA version or the absence of a required component in the SaaS setup, e.g. Joule in SAP SuccessFactors needs Employee Center. While these challenges are common, Clean Core principles and supporting practices help customers overcome such blockers, enabling them to modernise systems and keep pace with innovation - recognising that cloud transformation is a journey, not a quick fix (see another blog: A Case Study: Modernise the Development Practice with Clean Core Principles).

  • Starting Small with MVPs: Some use cases need to be built from scratch and beginning with “short and sweet” MVPs is the key. This approach focuses on verifying technological feasibility, like using Large Language Models (LLM) or Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) in Proof-of-Concepts (PoCs). The emphasis should be on agility and iterative progress rather than aiming for perfection in the first attempt.

  • Setting Proper Expectations and Adoption Models: Properly managing end-user expectations is critical to avoid disillusionment. Because AI models, even when fine-tuned based on user input, may lack flexibility if overfitted to specific data. Establishing a continuous feedback loop ensures models stay relevant and adaptable to the latest data and changing business needs.

In summary, enterprises are increasingly adopting AI in the business solutions. The hype from the personal AI use scenarios created the illusion that "AI will also solve every business problem", leading to frustrations and challenges for the expansion of adoption. While SAP has doubled down in AI for the business solutions, it will take time simply because the complexity, integration and ethical considerations involved. Zooming out, we see SAP striving to cross the chasm of AI adoption. With the task forces from the SAP engineering, delivery and partners exploring different approaches, the "best practices" are emerging. As momentum builds, the chasm will be crossed, and Business AI will become a standard component of "Business As Usual".

 

Diagram 1 reference: https://aesanetwork.org/beyond-rogers-diffusion-of-innovations-and-extension-curricula/

Diagram 2 reference: https://docs-eam.leanix.net/docs/pace-layering

entry.title: Architecting Email Approval without My Inbox for SAP Build Process Automation Workflows: Part 1
entry.content:
Business Context

While working day in and day out on SAP BTP use cases, we have noticed a growing inclination among customers towards “Automation” specially with SAP BTP SAP Build Process Automation. This trend has led to the development of various side-by-side custom extensions on SAP BTP utilizing SAP Build Process Automation.

Problem Statement

One of the most common requests we receive from multiple customers is for a mechanism that allows email-based approval without the need to manually log into the My Inbox application.

Currently, there is no out-of-the-box capability available to fulfill this requirement. Therefore, we will be developing our own reusable module to address this need.

In this three-part blog series, my colleague & BTP Architect @Adwait_Fadnavis  and I will provide insights on how to implement Email-based approval using SAP Build Process Automation capabilities.

We have prepared the content for this series in three blogs, where we will be diving deep into Design, Development, and Testing.

  • Blog 1 (This blog) – This blog will focus on the Business process, context, problem statement, end to end development flow, and required prerequisites.
  • Blog 2 This blog will focus on development and will provide a detailed walkthrough of the development process.
  • Blog 3: This blog will focus on complete end-to-end testing, helping to understand the overall objective.

Scenario Explanation

In this scenario, a workflow will be triggered using SAP Build Process Automation, which will send an email notification to the respective approver.

“The approver will receive the email notification and can reply to this email with either “Approve” or “Reject.” 

Based on the response, the corresponding workflow task will be approved or rejected.

Important Considerations

  1. Valid Approver Verification: The approver must be a valid approver, and the email must come from the approver’s registered email address. If the email does not come from the proper approver, or if the response is not “Approved” or “Reject,” the system must send an email back indicating that the response is not valid.
  2. Unique Request Number: Each approval request must generate a unique request number. This number will be used to validate the response against the original request, ensuring that the approval or rejection is correctly matched to the corresponding workflow task.

End to End Business Process Flow: Streamlining Approval Processes with SAP BPA Email-Based Automation

End to End Process Flow : Email based Approval

  1. Workflow Initiation

The process begins when an SAP BPA workflow is triggered. This could be due to various business events, such as a purchase order requiring approval.

  1. Sending Approval Work Items : Once triggered, the workflow sends approval work items to the approver’s SAP BTP My Inbox. Simultaneously, an email notification is dispatched to the approver.
  2. Email Notification Details :The email notification includes essential details like the “workflow instance ID” or “Custom Request Number” in the subject line. This ensures the approver & automation (which will elaborate later) can easily identify the request.
  3. Approver’s Response Options: The approver has two options to respond:
  • Log into SAP BTP My Inbox: Approvers can log in and approve or reject the request directly.
  • Email-Based Response: Approvers can reply to the email with “Approve” or “Reject.”

Background Automation

An SAP BPA-based automation runs continuously in the background, monitoring the responses sent by the approver. This automation checks if the response is from a valid approver and corresponds to a valid work item.

  1. Updating Work Items: If the response is valid, the automation updates the SAP BPA work item accordingly. This eliminates the need for the approver to log into My Inbox separately, streamlining the process.
  2. Completion Notification: Upon completion of the process, an approval email is sent to confirm the outcome. This ensures all stakeholders are informed of the decision.

Benefits of Email-Based Approval Automation

  • Efficiency: Reduces the time required for approvals by allowing responses directly via email.
  • Convenience: Approvers can respond from anywhere without needing to log into the system.
  • Accuracy: Automated monitoring ensures only valid responses are processed, reducing errors.

Initial Setup and Configuration for Inbound SMTP-Based Approval

Implementing an email-based approval system using SAP Build Process Automation (BPA) requires a few essential setup steps. Below steps will walk you through the initial configuration to ensure a smooth and efficient process.

Outbound SMTP Destination in SAP Build Process Automation

To send emails from SAP Build Process Automation to users’ email accounts, you need to configure an outbound SMTP destination. This setup is crucial for enabling the system to dispatch approval email notifications.

Inbound SMTP Email Configuration

For receiving email responses from approvers, we will use Google for both inbound and outbound SMTP. This setup can also be adapted for Microsoft email or other email services. Here are the key steps:

  1. Enable Access for Reading Gmail: Ensure that the Gmail account used for receiving responses has the necessary permissions enabled. For detailed instructions, refer to the relevant  sap documentation.

Save Credentials JSON File: After setting up the Google Cloud Platform(GCP), securely save the credentials. These credentials are necessary for the SAP BPA to access and read incoming emails.External User Setup in SAP BPA Control Tower: Complete the configuration for the external user in the SAP BPA Control Tower. This step ensures that the system can correctly route and process incoming emails from approvers.

SAP BTP BPA Control Tower

 

Setting Up External Authentication for the Google(Gmail)

Desktop Agent Installation and External User configuration (SMTP Inbound)

The Desktop Agent must be installed in unattended mode to run background processes efficiently.

It is recommended to install the Desktop Agent on a virtual machine to ensure continuous operation. Follow these steps:

Desktop Agent Sub Account Setup: Configure the Desktop Agent within the appropriate sub-account.

  1. Set to Unattended Mode: For real-time production scenarios, set the Desktop Agent to unattended mode. This setup is crucial for automating the approval process without manual intervention.
  2. Configure External User: Ensure that the external user configured in the previous step is correctly set up in the Desktop Agent. This configuration allows the agent to process email responses and update the SAP BPA work items accordingly.

Desktop Agent - Registering External Authentication

By following these steps, you are all set for robust email-based approval system using SAP BPA, streamlining your approval processes and enhancing efficiency.

Design and Development Walkthrough for SMTP Inbound Email-Based Approval Automation 

Once the initial setup and configuration are complete in the SAP BPA Control Tower and the unattended Desktop Agent is installed & configured, the next step is to design and develop the automation components.

Below is a summary of the key components involved in this process. My colleague @Adwait_Fadnavis  will provide a detailed walkthrough in a subsequent blog post.

The first step is to create a BPA automation. This involves several key actions:

  1. Invoke Google Authentication and Search for New Emails:

    • Authenticate External Gmail: Set up Google authentication to allow the system to access your Gmail account.
    • Search Gmail: Create a step to search for new, unread emails with the appropriate subject and request ID. Ensure that only unread emails are read to avoid processing duplicates.
    • Validate the Approver:

    • Check Valid Approver Validation : Validate that the response is from an authorized approver by reading the subject line for the request number.
    • Fetch Work Item Details: Use the SAP BPA API to retrieve work item details and match the approver. If the approver is not valid or authorized, send an error message.
    • Read the Email Body and Response:

    • Process Response: If the email response contains “Approve” or “OK,” call the SAP BPA API to update the workflow task accordingly.
    • Send Completion Email: Notify Stakeholders: After successfully processing the workflow approval or rejection, send an email notification to inform all relevant parties of the outcome.

In the upcoming blog post, @Adwait_Fadnavis  will delve into the development flow in detail and provide valuable insights on how to effectively develop inbound SMTP. Final Development flow chart will look as below:

End to End: Development FlowStay tuned for a comprehensive guide that will enhance your understanding and streamline your Development  process.

Regards,

Mohit Bansal, SAP BTP & S4HANA Architect

entry.title: Architecting Email Approval without My Inbox for SAP Build Process Automation Workflows: Part 3
entry.content:
This is the third and final blog of the series, in which we would like to explain the testing part in detail.

We have kept testing separate in this blog to provide a comprehensive explanation of all scenarios and steps from a technical perspective.

We have prepared the content for this series in three blogs, where we will be diving deep into Design, Development, and Testing.

  • Blog 1 – This blog will focus on the Business process, context, problem statement, end to end development flow, and required prerequisites.
  • Blog 2 This blog will focus on development and will provide a detailed walkthrough of the development process.
  • Blog 3(This blog) : This blog will focus on complete end-to-end testing, helping to understand the overall objective.

In the previous blog, @Adwait_Fadnavis  walked you through the complete development process. Now, I would like to start with the testing part.

Detailed Example of the Testing Process

To illustrate the testing process in more detail, let’s walk through an example:

  1. Automation Scheduling: The automation is scheduled to run every 15 minutes. The desktop agent is set up on a virtual machine to ensure it runs in unattended mode.
  2. Dummy Workflow Setup: A dummy workflow is created with fields such as “Request ID,” “Requester Name,” and “Approval Status.” Upon submission, an email is sent to the approver.
  3. Email Response Handling: The approver receives the email and responds with “Approve.” The automation script checks the email subject line and sender’s email address to validate the response.
    1. Invalid Response / Invalid Approver Handling: If the response is not valid or from an unauthorized approver, the system logs the incident and notifies the relevant parties for further investigation.
    2. Approver Handling: If the response is valid or from an authorized approver,  Workflow task will be approved.
    3. Rejection Scenario: If the approver responds with “Reject,” the workflow status is updated to “Rejected,” and an email notification is sent to the requester and other stakeholders.
  4. Workflow Update: The workflow status is updated to “Approved,” and an email notification is sent to the requester and other stakeholders.
  5. Sending Notification Emails: Email Notification to all stake holders involved.

Let's Deep dive above steps in Detail:

Step 1: Automation Scheduled as Background Job

In a real-time scenario, this automation can be scheduled as a background job at intervals of 15 minutes, 30 minutes, or any custom-defined time interval.

The frequency can be adjusted based on the volume of approval tasks. This scheduling ensures that the system checks for new approval requests regularly and processes them in a timely manner.

As mentioned in the first blog, it is mandatory to have the desktop agent running in unattended mode. This setup is crucial for ensuring that the automation runs smoothly without manual intervention.

For real-time business scenarios, we recommend installing the desktop agent on virtual machines to ensure continuous operation and to handle high volumes of approval tasks efficiently.

Job scheduling : SAP Build Process Automation

Step 2: (Optional) Triggering the Workflow -  Prepare the Dummy Workflow for Quick Testing

To facilitate quick testing, we prepared a small workflow using SAP Build Process Automation with a few fields. This dummy workflow simulates the real-time process and helps in validating the automation setup. Although in real-time, the workflow will be triggered via a valid application, here is a snapshot of our dummy workflow for demonstration purposes:

  • Workflow Trigger: Upon clicking the submit button, the workflow is triggered.
  • Email Notification: An email is sent to the approver for approval. This step involves configuring an SMTP outbound email, as mentioned in the previous blog post.

This setup allows us to test the end-to-end process in a controlled environment before deploying it in a live scenarioriggering the Workflow -  Prepare the Dummy Workflow for Quick Testing (Optional)

Test for Sending Email for Task Creation

Sample Email Body

Triggering Workflow : Testing Demonstrations:

Process Triggered and Sent for Approval

 Step 3: Handling Email Responses

Upon receiving the email, the approver will have three options: a) Approve b) Reject c) Not a valid response

Let’s say the approver starts with “ok” and responds with this in the email.

Upon receiving the response, our automation running as a background job will check if the response is coming from a valid approver and if the subject line is correct (the subject line should not be modified).

This validation step is crucial to ensure that only authorized responses are processed.

Validating Correct ApproverValidating Correct Approvers Response will be send if the Approver is not valid.

Response will be send if the Approver is not valid.

Validating Correct response: Validating Correct response

Step 4: Workflow Update on Approval

If the work item is approved, the workflow will move forward and update the task accordingly. This involves:

  • Updating the Workflow Status: The status of the workflow is updated to “Approved” in the system

Workflow Update on Approval

  • Notifying Stakeholders: All relevant stakeholders are notified about the approval via email.

Notifying Stakeholders

Workflow Update on Rejection

Similarly, if the response is “no” or “Rejected,” the work item will be updated, and the workflow will proceed with the rejected branch. This involves:

  • Updating the Workflow Status: The status of the workflow is updated to “Rejected” in the system.

Updating the Workflow Rejection Status

  • Notifying Stakeholders: All relevant stakeholders are notified about the rejection via email.

Step 5: Sending Notification Emails

An email notification will be sent to all involved parties to inform them of the status of the workflow request. This step ensures transparency and keeps everyone informed about the progress of the approval process.

rejection via email

We hope you enjoyed reading our blog series by me and @Adwait_Fadnavis  on Architecting Email Approval for SAP Build Process Automation Workflows. We trust that this approach provides valuable insights into managing workflow approvals efficiently without relying on manual My Inbox / Task Center approvals.

Thank you for following along with us.

Regards,

Mohit Bansal, SAP BTP & S/4HANA Architect

entry.title: Difference in Value
entry.content:
Hi
 
I have all decimals with 2 places . Quantity has 3. Problem i am facing suppose a/p invoice value is 50000. When copies to Credit note it does not show exact value. There is difference of some paisa. Why is it so.
 
Thanks

entry.title: Re: Difference in Value
entry.content: The difference in value when copying an A/P invoice to a credit note is likely due to rounding inconsistencies between the two. This can happen when tax or line item totals are rounded differently, or if the system applies different decimal precision at each stage. Check your software's rounding settings and ensure consistency across invoices and credit notes. If it's a small difference, manual adjustment might be needed.

entry.title: Event based integrations in S/4HANA Cloud using Event Mesh for Integration Suite (EMIS)
entry.content:
In this blog post, I would like to cover the concept of event-based integration in S/4HANA Cloud. Fundamentally, there are three different services offered by SAP to achieve event-based integrations:

  • SAP Event Mesh (standalone): Documentation
  • SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh (AEM): Documentation
  • Event Mesh in SAP Integration Suite (a.k.a EMIS): Documentation

SAP Event Mesh is a standalone service offered by SAP, which can be used by customers with existing CPEA contract models. For newer customers with BTPEA contract models, the standalone service is no longer supported and customers can choose between AEM and EMIS. EMIS is embedded within the Integration Suite and can be activated without further license costs, whereas AEM is licensed separately and is geared to handle much higher data loads and comes with some more advanced capabilities.

In this blog post, I’ll be demonstrating a simple scenario of how to get started with event-based integrations using EMIS as a starting point. For a detailed background on EMIS, please refer to Karsten Strothman’s great blog post on the topic.

Prerequisites:

BTP Subaccount:

  • Add the entitlement for the EMIS service
  • In the subaccount instances and subscriptions page, create an instance as follows:

  •  

  • Within the instance, create a new service binding:

  •  

  • After creation, click on ‘View Credentials’, where you can note down the values of the clientid, uri and token endpoint fields, which will later be used in the communication system setup on S/4HANA Cloud side
    • For the uri field, only the string between wss:// and /protocols/amqp10ws needs to be copied
    • In this step, also copy the entire JSON shown below, which will later be used as a service key

  •  

 

S/4HANA Cloud:

  • Activate scope item 1NN.
  • Create the communication system, outbound communication user and the Communication Arrangement SAP_COM_0092 (refer here)
  • While creating a new communication system, in the host name, enter the uri string value copied from above.
    • Under OAuth 2.0 Settings, populate the values for authorization endpoint and token endpoint. For token endpoint, use the value copied from above. For authorization endpoint, use the same value as the token endpoint, simply replacing /token with /authorize
    • Under ‘Users for Inbound Communication’, create a new communication user or select an existing one (User/Password) for the inbound communication.
    • Under ‘Users for Outbound Communication’, choose OAuth 2.0 and enter the Client ID and secret copied from earlier
  • Now, create a new communication arrangement for the scenario SAP_COM_0092:
    • Now, paste the value from the “Copy JSON” step earlier. After pasting, add one property manually which specifies the namespace of the comm. arrangement

    •  

  • After this, the outbound service to delivery events from S/4HANA Cloud is setup. Now it’s time to choose a particular event object to test the event delivery.
  • For this, first create a channel binding in the app “Enterprise Event Enablement - Configure Channel Binding”. Here, you will find the channel name from your communication arrangement.
    • Under Outbound Topic Bindings, select create, and add the event object that you would like to send events for. For my example, I will choose cost center. Be aware that per object, you need to select the add the required event type (create/update/delete)

    •  

The last setup step is on the Cloud Integration (a.k.a CPI) side. Here under the Configure Event Mesh section, create a new queue for the test:

 

Inside the queue, add a subscription, where you will need to paste the following value: <Topic Space>/ce/<Outbound Topic Binding>. The <Topic Space> value is found in the communication arrangement. In my example, the final value will look something like this:

 

 

 

 

Integration Test:

Now that the necessary setup steps are complete, it’s time to test if the event is correctly being sent. Since my example topic binding is for the cost center change event, I will use the “Manage Cost Centers” app to change the value of an example cost center.

After doing this, we can see that the event has been sent out successfully and is now queued within the EMIS message queue in CPI:

 

 

With this, we have concluded the setup steps necessary to configure the technical communication between S/4HANA Cloud and Event Mesh for Integration Suite, in order to work with event-based integrations. Hope this gave you a comprehensive overview as to what configuration steps are required in the various systems in order to have the technical communication between the systems up and running. After this initial setup, integration flows will usually be developed within CPI in order to consume these events, put together the necessary payloads, and send the required data to external systems for consumption.

entry.title: Discover the SAP Fiori Launchpad
entry.content:
The SAP Fiori launchpad is the entry point for many SAP solutions, such as SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP ABAP environment. 

As a business user, it provides you with intuitive access to the apps assigned to you based on your job role(s). Here you can find everything you need to get your on-system tasks done, on the device that best suits your task.

This user experience is not just about apps. The launchpad includes a range of useful features across all your apps so you can work smarter, such as AI (Joule, your digital assistant), human-to-human collaboration tools, app-to-app navigation, search by object ids, set your preferred defaults, notifications of urgent work, context-sensitive help, set your personal preferences on your home page, and more. 

SAP Fiori launchpad for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
*** Please note: Which apps and features are available will depend on your SAP solution and your access is controlled by your administrator(s).

Key Features

SAP Fiori launchpad and its features bring the end-to-end experience of the user together, with scalable options for simple and complex roles, whether you are deploying a few apps or thousands. 

The launchpad allows business users to launch their apps on mobile and desktop devices, giving them all the relevant information they need at a glance. In addition, the launchpad provides all apps with services such as navigation, personalization, embedded support, and application configuration.

Please note that users can launch apps of different UI types, not only SAP Fiori apps but also classic UIs (GUI transactions in SAP GUI for HTML, ABAP Web Dynpro applications, Web Client UIs), and extended content such as remote content - e.g. custom-built apps on BTP,  SAP Analytics Cloud stories, etc. 

This makes the launchpad an ideal place to manage process changes over time for your business users. For example, gradually move your users from GUI transactions to SAP Fiori apps, using the launchpad layouts (spaces and pages) to highlight recommended versus old apps during transition and warn which apps will be removed soon.

Navigate with Spaces and Pages

The spaces and pages navigation menu is how you access your most important apps in a structured, business-meaningful way. The spaces represent a work area, while pages a work context bundling most used and relevant applications in sections for the business user.

Once you have started working in an app, the launchpad enables app-to-app navigation, so you don't need to go back to the launchpad to find your next app. Take a look at the navigation and check out short demos in the blog Getting Started with Spaces and Pages. 

The SAP Fiori launchpad for different products

The SAP Fiori launchpad is not a product you can buy separately, you get it together with different SAP solutions, such as SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA, SAP BTP ABAP environment and Integrated Business Planning. 

Finding information about the launchpad for your solution:

  • For all supported SAP solutions:
    • SAP Fiori launchpad | SAP Help Portal lists the SAP Help documentation for all solutions and all releases, with dedicated guides for users, administrators and developers.
    • For a brief structured overview of SAP Fiori launchpad, see SAP Fiori Launchpad | SAP Fiori for Web Design Guidelines. 
    • Discover the world of SAP Fiori in SAP Fiori Overview | SAP Help Portal.
  • For SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the tutorial SAP Fiori Launchpad guides users on a first tour around the launchpad and the blog Getting Started with Spaces and Pages is a good starting point for further exploration.
  • For SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and for SAP S/4HANA, check out the community SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA | SAP Community.
    • Technical names: In the SAP Fiori launchpad | SAP Help Portal, you find the SAP Fiori launchpad for SAP S/4HANA 2023 under the technical name SAP Fiori launchpad in ABAP platform 2023 with SAP_UI 758.
    • Deployment options: You can deploy the SAP Fiori launchpad on the same system as your S/4HANA ('embedded deployment') or on a separate server. SAP recommends embedded deployment. For technical details about the different deployment options, see SAP Fiori deployment options and SAP Fiori front-end server.  
    • Administration guide: Administrators are supported with a detailed Administration guide, covering the initial setup, configuration, launchpad content setup, integration and operations, including the launchpad support tools, 
    • Step-by-step tutorial: Administrators can follow the demo guide SAP Fiori Rapid Activation & Content Management, which is part of the series SAP S/4HANA Fully-Activated Appliance: Demo Guides.  
  • For some other SAP cloud solutions, such as SAP BTP, ABAP environment or SAP Integrated Business Planning, the SAP Fiori launchpad also serves as product-specific entry point and is described as part of the solution, e.g. Administration and Operations in the ABAP Environment | SAP Help Portal.
  • SAP Business Suite systems are supported with SAP Fiori front-end server 2022, for technical details and deployment options, see SAP Fiori deployment options and SAP Fiori front-end server - SAP Community. 

Adaptation and Extensibility 

For both cloud and on-premise products, SAP offers extension possibilities for key users and for developers.

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: (once 2502 is available, I will add a link to the new S/4 Cloud Developer Guide | SAP Help Portal)  

    • Key users and developers can use key user adaptation to easily make common changes and make the adapted apps available to all users. With key user adaptation, it’s possible to rearrange fields, change field labels, add fields or simple logic, hide existing fields and features, or embed external content, such as maps. Unlike personalization, changes made through key user adaptation apply to all app users. You can even publish UI changes as a separate app, known as an app variant, making it available to other users. For more information, see Adapting SAP Fiori UIs at Runtime - Key User Adaptation.

    • If you need to make more complex changes, such as adding fragments to extension points or aggregations, developers can extend an application using developer adaptation. For an overview of possible adaptations, see Developer Adaptation, and for more detailed information, see Extending SAP Fiori Applications.

    • When a new custom app is required, developers can build SAP Fiori applications for an OData service and make it accessible to business users from the SAP Fiori launchpad, see UI Development.

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA:
    • Key users and developers can use key user adaptation to easily make common changes and make the adapted apps available to all users. Check out the options for key users Adapting SAP Fiori UIs at Runtime - Key User Adaptation
    • If you need to make more complex changes, such as adding fragments to extension points or aggregations, developers can extend an application using developer adaptation. For an overview of possible adaptations, see Developer Adaptation, and for more detailed information, see Extending SAP Fiori Applications.  
    • When a new custom app is required or if you want to enhance the launchpad with custom features, developers can follow the Developer Guide.
  • SAP BTP, ABAP Environment offers the developer guide SAP Fiori Applications in the ABAP Environment.  

SAP Fiori launchpad, SAP Build Work Zone and SAP Mobile Start

For users that work mainly with one SAP solution, for example SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, the SAP Fiori launchpad offers a personalized entry point to the world of SAP S/4HANA. 

SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition, on the other hand, can serve as central entry point to more than one SAP system - and of course you can reuse launchpad content from your SAP Fiori launchpad ('automatic content federation'). For more information, see SAP Build Work Zone | SAP Community.

SAP Mobile Start brings launchpad apps and capabilities to extended device types, such as wearables (smartwatches), foldable phones and Apple Vision Pro.

entry.title: Re: SAP CAP nodeJS .drafts undefined
entry.content:
Hi @T1mey ,

Have you thought about providing more information and/or code example on how your model, service and the SELECT statement looks like? In my experience, in most of the cases this may speed up the process of finding out what the exact case is.

I tried with "@sap/cds": "^8.5.0" in the following ways, both worked.

service implementation variants:

 

class MyService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  init() {
    const { MyServiceEntity } = this.entities;

    (async () => {
      const res = await SELECT.from(MyServiceEntity.drafts);
    })();

    this.on('READ', MyServiceEntity, async (req) => {

      await SELECT.from(MyServiceEntity.drafts);

    });
module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function (srv) {
  
  const { MyServiceEntity } = this.entities;

  (async () => {
    const res = await SELECT.from(MyServiceEntity.drafts);
  })();

  srv.on('READ', MyServiceEntity, async (req) => {

    await SELECT.from(MyServiceEntity.drafts);

  });

 

service.cds

 

using { my.namespace as db } from '../db/schema';

service MyService {
    entity MyServiceEntity as projection on db.MyDbEntity;
}

annotate MyService.MyServiceEntity with @odata.draft.enabled;

 

db.schema

 

entity MyDbEntity {
  key ID: Integer;
}

 

Regards,
Peter
